%\chapter*{Abstract}
\phantomsection
\addtotoc{Abstract}

\begin{center}\huge \textbf{Abstract}\end{center}

The exponential growth in data demand for high-performance computing and graphics processing units (GPUs) necessitates robust high-speed serial interconnects. This project focuses on the design of a PCI Express (PCIe) Serializer/Deserializer (SERDES) Physical Layer (PHY), specifically targeting the Receiver Analog Front-End (Rx AFE). As PCIe standards evolve from Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ) to Pulse Amplitude Modulation 4-level (PAM4) signaling in Gen 6.0, the design challenges regarding signal integrity, bandwidth, and linearity increase significantly.

This thesis presents a comprehensive literature review and theoretical analysis of critical Rx AFE blocks, including termination networks, bandwidth extension techniques, and Variable Gain Amplifiers (VGAs). We analyze various bandwidth extension topologies, such as T-coils and bridged-shunt peaking, and evaluate VGA architectures for linearity and dynamic range to propose an optimal architecture for modern PCIe standards.

A Continuous-Time Linear Equalizer (CTLE) is an analog equalization block used in high-speed receivers to compensate for frequency-dependent channel loss by boosting high-frequency signal components. It operates in continuous time, typically using RC degeneration or active feedback, providing low-latency and power-efficient equalization. CTLEs are effective at mitigating linear ISI and are usually made programmable to adapt to different channel conditions, though their performance is limited by noise amplification.

A Decision Feedback Equalizer (DFE) is a nonlinear equalizer that cancels inter-symbol interference by subtracting the weighted contribution of previously detected symbols from the current decision. Because it relies on past decisions rather than linear filtering, a DFE can remove post-cursor ISI without amplifying noise. Its main challenges are feedback loop latency and error propagation, especially at very high data rates, but it remains essential in modern high-speed serial receivers.
